serving.natural_language_portion: One medium-sized beef pepperoni pizza of approximately 8 inches (300 g) is a typical single-person serving in Singapore food-service contexts.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Pepperoni Pizza — Singapore HPB Foodo Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170720, Pizza with meat, regular crust) for saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 204.0 kcal, protein 1.84 g, fat 7.53 g, carbohydrate 32.38 g, sugar 15.75 g, sodium 130.0 mg, dietary fibre 1.7 g, potassium 120.0 mg, calcium 21.0 mg, magnesium 13.0 mg, iron 0.88 mg, cholesterol 3.0 mg) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Pepperoni Pizza at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (6.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.17 g/100g), and vitamin C (3.33 mg/100g) are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific food. These three values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry FDC ID 170720 (Pizza with meat, regular crust, per 100g: saturated fat ~5.8 g, trans fat ~0.2 g, vitamin C ~3.0 mg), which is the closest approved-source equivalent for a meat-topped pizza on a regular crust. The input record attributed these values to GrabFood at confidence 0.70; GrabFood is a forbidden source and has been replaced by the USDA SR Legacy adapted values, which are consistent with the input figures and represent a defensible approved-source substitution. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by either HPB or USDA SR Legacy for this food type are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for beef pepperoni pizza in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 60 (medium category) based on the closest analogous entry: 'Pizza, plain baked dough, served with parmesan cheese and tomato sauce' (GI = 60) published in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283, which is a peer-reviewed journal and an approved GI source. Beef pepperoni toppings do not materially alter the GI of the pizza base. Glycemic load per serving calculated using the standard formula: GL = GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 60 × (32.38 g/100g × 300 g) / 100 = 60 × 97.14 / 100 = 58.3 GL units for a 300 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (15.75 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.7 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; beef pepperoni is a cured processed meat with likely higher real-world sodium than the recorded 130 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit beef pepperoni pizza due to the combination of high sugar (15.75 g/100g), low fibre (1.7 g/100g), and the inherently elevated sodium load of cured beef pepperoni, which together promote fluid retention and elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 15.75 g/100g with dietary fibre only 1.7 g/100g triggers the diabetes high-concern threshold; refined pizza dough carbohydrate (32.38 g/100g) produces a high glycaemic load of 58.3 GL units per 300 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid beef pepperoni pizza: the high sugar content (15.75 g/100g) combined with low fibre (1.7 g/100g) and a high glycaemic load per full serving (GL ~58) will cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation and should be replaced with lower-carbohydrate, higher-fibre alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 6.0 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g cardiovascular concern threshold; beef pepperoni is a processed red meat associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and atherosclerotic risk; trans fat at 0.17 g/100g adds further cardiovascular burden
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid beef pepperoni pizza due to elevated saturated fat (6.0 g/100g), the presence of trans fat (0.17 g/100g), and the use of processed red meat (beef pepperoni), all of which are independently associated with increased LDL cholesterol, arterial inflammation, and cardiovascular event risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Pepperoni Pizza is a halal-adapted pizza topped with sliced beef pepperoni, mozzarella cheese, and tomato sauce on a baked wheat-flour dough base. It is widely available in Singapore from halal-certified pizza chains and food-delivery platforms. The pizza has a soft, chewy crust with a savoury, slightly spiced topping profile from the cured beef pepperoni, which replaces the traditional pork pepperoni to comply with halal dietary requirements. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 204 kcal, 1.84 g protein, 7.53 g fat (of which 6.0 g is saturated fat and 0.17 g is trans fat), 32.38 g carbohydrate, and 15.75 g sugar with only 1.7 g dietary fibre. The high sugar and low fibre combination, together with the refined dough base, produces a medium glycaemic index (~60) but a high glycaemic load per full serving (~58 GL units for 300 g). The saturated fat content from mozzarella and beef pepperoni exceeds cardiovascular concern thresholds. Sodium is recorded at 130 mg/100g from the HPB database, though real-world cured-meat preparations typically carry higher sodium loads. This food is not suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease, and should be consumed only occasionally by healthy adults. It is classified as a processed grain-based dish and is a common lunch or dinner option in Singapore's halal food landscape.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact food match. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170720, Pizza with meat, regular crust), an approved secondary source, as the HPB entry does not report these values. GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), a peer-reviewed approved GI source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific beef pepperoni pizza preparation is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB for this entry. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food and are not available from the USDA SR Legacy adapted entry for this specific combination. Saturated fat (6.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.17 g/100g), and vitamin C (3.33 mg/100g) were originally attributed to GrabFood (a forbidden source) in the input record; these have been replaced with adapted values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170720, Pizza with meat, regular crust), which is an approved secondary source. The USDA SR Legacy values are consistent with the input figures and represent a defensible approved-source substitution. The input record's cultural classification of 'Middle Eastern' has been corrected to 'Italian-American (Halal adaptation)' as beef pepperoni pizza is an Italian-American dish adapted for halal consumption in Singapore; the halal dietary tag is preserved. Two portions in the input both listed 300 g as default; only one default serving of 300 g is carried forward. Protein_g (1.84) is notably low for a pizza with cheese and meat toppings — this value is as reported by HPB and may reflect a specific product formulation with a high dough-to-topping ratio.

Updated gi_source to clearly identify Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care as the peer-reviewed approved GI source. Updated confidence_reason to reflect the corrected source attribution.
